mnemonics: Can TSP-SALESMAN Find Very Good Routes? (Complete, TSP, Symmetric, Asymmetric, Loop, Eulerian, Matrix, Flow, Vertices, Graph, Reachability, Walk)
keyPoints: The Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) aims to find the shortest possible route that visits each vertex exactly once and returns to the origin.
A directed graph (digraph) consists of vertices connected by edges with a specific direction, unlike undirected graphs.

Key digraph types include: Symmetric (edges go both ways), Asymmetric (one-way edges), Tournaments (one edge between every pair), Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) with no cycles, and Strongly Connected digraphs (a path exists between every pair of vertices).
commonMistakes: Assuming the TSP is the same as finding a minimum spanning tree, which does not require a cycle or visiting each node exactly once.
Treating a directed graph as undirected, which ignores the crucial constraint of edge direction in problems like pathfinding or flow.
Assuming a digraph is strongly connected without verifying a path exists between every ordered pair of vertices (u,v) AND (v,u).
